topic: Practising the Passive Voice in Arguments
title: Using the Passive Voice for Formal Arguments
explanation_chunks: This page helps you practice using the passive voice, especially when writing about arguments. We'll continue looking at the topic of whether on-screen entertainment is good for children.

First, you'll organize different ideas about on-screen entertainment into a 'for' and 'against' table. Remember to think of examples or reasons to support each point, just like we learned for balanced arguments.

Next, you'll complete sentences using the passive voice by choosing the correct verb. This helps you get used to how the passive voice changes the structure of a sentence, putting the action's receiver first.

You'll also get to change active sentences into passive ones. Remember, to make a sentence passive, you take the object of the active sentence and make it the new subject, then use a 'to be' verb and the past participle of the main verb.

Using the passive voice can make your arguments sound more formal and academic, which is often useful in non-fiction writing. Phrases like 'It is believed that...' or 'It is said that...' are great examples of how passive voice makes sentences sound more objective.
examples: Active: 'People say that...' Passive: 'It is said that...'
Active: 'Traffic causes pollution.' Passive: 'Pollution is caused by traffic.'
Active: 'Parents need a break from supervising children.' (Reason for on-screen entertainment).
Against: 'Watching too much TV means that children spend too much time indoors.'
key_points: Practice sorting arguments into 'for' and 'against' categories.
Reinforce understanding of active to passive voice transformation.
Use passive voice to achieve a more formal tone in writing.
Passive voice allows you to focus on the action or its result, rather than the doer.
Common passive phrases like 'It is believed that...' are useful in arguments.

vocabulary.definition: Annoyed or bothered (someone) with frequent interruptions or requests.
Had an effect on the character, development, or behaviour of someone or something.
Language that is appropriate for serious or official situations, often using precise words and complex sentence structures.
Equal in value, amount, function, or meaning.

explain_again: This page is like a practice playground for using the passive voice. You get to play with sentences, changing them around so that what happens becomes the main focus, instead of who did it. It's also about figuring out how to make your arguments sound extra smart and grown-up by using these special sentence structures.
fun_analogy: Using the passive voice for formal writing is like putting on your best suit or dress for a special occasion. You're still you, but your outfit (your sentence structure) makes you look more polished and serious, perfect for talking about important topics!
